text: Good to see you here. We've had an exchange on twitter that encouraged me to pursue an idea around smartphone-friendly end-user programming, so thank you for that :)

Hi everyone! I found out about this slack via <@U5TCAFTD3> on twitter (thank you!) I'm currently on a break from consulting to focus on a personal project that's been cooking in my head for the past 5 years. It's a predictive, end-user programmable (forth-like), on-screen keyboard with variable key count (1–N) for use with assistive devices, smartphones, and VR input devices like tapwithus. Happy to learn from such a great community :)
Hi, sounds really interesting! Do you have any demo videos, blog posts or a public repo? Would love to take a look at it!

this is insanely inspiring. stop tempting me to move to SF!!

>>>Dark runs interpreters in the cloud. When you write new code in a function or HTTP/event handler, we send a diff of the Abstract Syntax Tree (the representation of your code that our editor and servers use under the hood) to our servers, and then we run that code when requests come in. So deployment is just a small write to a database, which is instant and atomic. Our deploys are so fast because we trimmed a deploy to the smallest thing possible.

I have not but I like what I see and am going to grab a copy of your Thesis. Thanks for sharing!

What I like a lot from reading this article, is that with dark, when you are editing something you actually modify the "running" system. And as the dark environment is aware of feature (why you modify) and version (what your modification is changing), it is safe to do it and test it "live" and then deploy it very quickly when you are confident with your modification. In fact, the system is centered on what your intention is (as a programmer) and you can focus on it and not on the tools, and the system is ready to commit your changes with ease.

Big fan on the 68k (once wrote an entire compiler in 68k asm), but blaming crappier designs winning on "job security" is a bit simple, imho. In many of these cases, the market simply does not care for elegant (to the programmer), it cares about cost, right place right time, and lucky connections to other tech / other companies etc.
Intel managed to make the iterations of the 386 faster (than the 68k) and cheaper (than the risc alternatives), with its horrible ISA never much of a disadvantage

Thoughts on this?<https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Power_of_10:_Rules_for_Developing_Safety-Critical_Code>

attachments.title: The Power of 10: Rules for Developing Safety-Critical Code
attachments.text: The Power of 10 Rules were created in 2006 by Gerard J. Holzmann of the NASA/JPL Laboratory for Reliable Software.  The rules are intended to eliminate certain C coding practices which make code difficult to review or statically analyze.  These rules are a complement to the MISRA C guidelines and have been incorporated into the greater set of JPL coding standards.
attachments.fallback: wikipedia: The Power of 10: Rules for Developing Safety-Critical Code
